items.title: Definition and diagnostic criteria
items.contentHtml:
Definition and diagnostic criteria

  • Endometriosis is the presence of endometrial tissue (glands and stroma) outside the uterine cavity, with a chronic inflammatory response.
  • The gold standard diagnostic criterion is laparoscopic visualization with histological confirmation of endometrial glands and stroma in typical lesions (red, black implants, vesicles clear).
  • The clinical criteria include progressive dysmenorrhea, profound dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain and infertility; Physical examination may reveal nodules in the uterosacral ligaments or a retroverted fixed uterus.
  • There is no diagnostic serum biomarker; CA-125 may be elevated but is nonspecific.

items.title: Retrograde menstruation theory
items.contentHtml:
Theory of retrograde menstruation

  • Proposed by Sampson in 1927: during menstruation, viable endometrial cells reflux through the tubes into the peritoneal cavity, where they implant and proliferate.
  • It is the most accepted theory, but it does not explain all cases (endometriosis in women with primary amenorrhea or in men with estrogenic treatment).
  • Factors that favor reflux: obstruction of menstrual flow (cervical stenosis, imperforate hymen), copious menstrual flow or immunological dysfunction that prevents the elimination of ectopic cells.
  • It is complemented by theories of coelomic metaplasia (transformation of the peritoneal epithelium) and lymphatic/hematogenous dissemination for distant lesions.

items.title: Ovarian endometriomas
items.contentHtml:
Ovarian endometriomas and their management

  • Endometriomas are ovarian cysts filled with thick chocolate cysts, formed by invagination of the ovarian cortex with endometriotic implants.
  • On transvaginal ultrasound they are seen as unilocular cysts with echogenicity. homogeneous in frosted glassand thin walls; on MRI they present hyperintense signal on T1 and hypointense signal on T2 (“shading”).
  • First-line medical management includes combined oral contraceptives or continuous progestins to suppress ovulation and reduce the size of the endometrioma.
  • laparoscopic cystectomy is indicated if the endometrioma measures >4 cm, is symptomatic or in patients with infertility; The ovarian cortex must be preserved so as not to affect the follicular reserve (risk of ovarian damage).

items.title: Adenomyosis vs endometriosis
items.contentHtml:
Adenomyosis vs endometriosis

  • Adenomyosis: presence of endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium, with adjacent smooth muscle hypertrophy. It causes menorrhagia, dysmenorrhea and an enlarged, globular and soft uterus.
  • Endometriosis: endometrial tissue outside the uterus (peritoneum, ovaries, ligaments). Cyclic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, infertility.
  • Both can coexist (up to 20-40% of cases). MRI is the best image to differentiate them: in adenomyosis, thickening of the junctional area (>12 mm) is observed, while endometriosis shows peritoneal implants or endometriomas.
  • Medical treatment is similar (NSAIDs, contraceptives, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists), but adenomyosis may require embolization or hysterectomy as definitive.

items.title: ASRM Classification
items.contentHtml:
ASRM Classification (stages)

  • The American Society for Reproductive Medicine (ASRM) classifies endometriosis into 4 stages according to intraoperative scoring: Stage I (minimum): 1-5 points; Stage II (mild): 6-15 points; Stage III (moderate): 16-40 points; Stage IV (severe): >40 points.
  • Evaluated: peritoneal implants (size and depth), ovarian endometriomas (size and rupture), and adhesions (grade and location: adnexal, posterior cul-de-sac).
  • The main limitation of the ASRM classification is that it does not correlate well with pain or fertility; It is mainly used to standardize surgical findings.
  • There are complementary classifications: ENZIAN (for deep endometriosis) and EFI (Endometriosis Fertility Index) which predicts the post-surgical spontaneous pregnancy rate.

items.title: Classic symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Symptoms: dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility

  • Secondary dysmenorrhea (progressive, worsening with age, begins days before menstruation and persists throughout bleeding).
  • Deep dyspareunia (pain during deep vaginal penetration, typically in endometriosis of the posterior fornix or ligaments uterosacral).
  • Infertility associated with mechanical factors (tubo-ovarian adhesions), inflammatory factors (adverse peritoneal microenvironment for gametes and embryo) and decreased ovarian reserve (endometriomas).
  • Other symptoms: cyclical dyschezia (pain when defecating during menstruation), cyclical dysuria, chronic pelvic pain and fatigue. The classic triad is dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia and infertility.

items.title: Diagnostic imaging
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic imaging: ultrasound and MRI

  • Transvaginal ultrasound is the first line: it detects endometriomas (cyst with ground glass echo, without internal vascularization), nodules in the rectovaginal septum and signs of adhesions ("kissing ovary" sign, fixed uterus in retroversion).
  • Magnetic resonance imaging is superior for Deep endometriosis: evaluate involvement of the uterosacral ligaments, posterior cul-de-sac, bladder, ureters and rectum. Findings: hyperintense implants on T1 with fat suppression, “shading” on T2.
  • The “muscle layer” sign on MRI (thickening of the junctional zone) points to associated adenomyosis.
  • Transrectal ultrasound can be useful to evaluate the rectovaginal septum and the rectal wall in cases of suspected endometriosis. deep.

items.title: Diagnostic laparoscopy
items.contentHtml:
Laparoscopy as the gold standard

  • Laparoscopy is the gold standard procedure for the definitive diagnosis of endometriosis, allowing direct visualization of implants, endometriomas and adhesions, as well as taking a biopsy for histological confirmation.
  • Typical lesions include red (active), black (old), white (scarred) implants and clear vesicles. ASRM classification is performed during laparoscopy.
  • It is recommended to systematically inspect: posterior fornix, uterosacral ligaments, ovaries, bladder peritoneum and appendix.
  • Negative laparoscopy (without visible lesions) does not completely rule out microscopic endometriosis; In cases of high suspicion, random peritoneal biopsy can be performed.

items.title: Initial medical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Medical treatment: NSAIDs and contraceptives

  • First-line non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for pain: they inhibit the synthesis of prostaglandins, reducing dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain. They are administered on a scheduled basis (not on demand) during menstruation.
  • Combined oral contraceptives (ethinyl estradiol + progestogen) in a continuous regimen (without pause) suppress ovulation, induce decidualization of ectopic endometrial tissue and reduce the progression of the disease.
  • Alternatives: progestogens (acetate medroxyprogesterone, dienogest, levonorgestrel in intrauterine device) that induce endometrial atrophy.
  • dienogest (2 mg/day) is a specific progestogen for endometriosis, with comparable efficacy to gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists but with fewer hypoestrogenic adverse effects.

items.title: GnRH agonists and antagonists
items.contentHtml:
GnRH agonists and antagonists

  • Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonists (leuprolide, goserelin, triptorelin): initially they stimulate the release of gonadotropins (flare effect), then they induce down-regulation of pituitary receptors, producing a reversible hypoestrogenic state (medical castration).
  • They are used for 3-6 months for severe or preoperative pain; require add-back therapy with estrogens + progestogens to prevent loss of bone density if prolonged >6 months.
  • Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) antagonists (cetrorelix, ganirelix, and the oral elagolix approved in the United States): they competitively block pituitary receptors without a flare effect, producing rapid and reversible suppression of gonadotropins.
  • Oral elagolix (150 mg/day or 200 mg twice a day) reduces pain in endometriosis; It can be associated with add-back therapy with estradiol and norethindrone acetate to mitigate bone loss.

items.title: Surgical management and fertility
items.contentHtml:
Surgical management and fertility preservation

  • Laparoscopic conservative surgery (excision of implants, cystectomy of endometriomas, lysis of adhesions) is indicated in patients with pain refractory to medical treatment or infertility due to endometriosis.
  • In endometriomas >4 cm, cystectomy is preferred over drainage and coagulation, although it carries a risk of decreased ovarian reserve (healthy cortex must be preserved).
  • To preserve fertility, early surgery is recommended in stages III-IV and referral to assisted reproduction if the patient desires pregnancy; in vitro fertilization (IVF) is an effective option even with severe endometriosis.
  • Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is curative but is only considered in patients with intractable pain who have completed their reproductive desire.

items.title: Association with ovarian cancer
items.contentHtml:
Association with ovarian cancer

  • Endometriosis is associated with an increased risk of epithelial ovarian cancer, especially of the clear cell and endometrioid subtypes (odds ratio ~2-3).
  • The proposed mechanism is chronic inflammation, oxidative stress and mutations in genes such as ARID1A and PIK3CA in foci of atypical endometriosis (precursor lesions).
  • The absolute risk is low (approximately 1-2% of women with endometriosis will develop ovarian cancer), so systematic screening or prophylactic surgery is not recommended.
  • Protective factors: combined oral contraceptives (they reduce the risk of ovarian cancer in general) and parity. Malignancy should be suspected in the presence of an endometrioma with rapid growth, papillae or solid areas on ultrasound.
